summary: The post critiques 'people analytics' tools that claim to objectively evaluate employees and ideas using algorithms. O'Neil argues that these systems don't achieve objectivity but instead encode existing biases from historical data into mathematical models. Using Royal Dutch Shell's idea-vetting algorithm as an example, she shows how training on 'previous winners' embeds whatever preferences and biases existed in the original human process. She contrasts this with truly blind orchestra auditions, where evaluation is based on a single transparent criterion. The result is companies that unknowingly perpetuate homogeneity while believing they've achieved objectivity.
key_points: Algorithms trained on 'previous winners' encode whatever biases existed in the original selection process
True blind evaluation (like orchestra auditions) requires commitment to a single, transparent criterion—not comparison to past successes
These hiring algorithms are unlikely to obviously fail because they efficiently find people similar to existing successful employees
The fundamental problem is transparency: black-box algorithms obscure what criteria are actually being used
Companies using these tools will increasingly hire people just like those they already have, reinforcing homogeneity
money_quotes: Whatever their process used to be, it's now embedded in an algorithm.
They might very well pass on really excellent ideas that happened to have come from a modest person – no discussion necessary on what kind of people are being invisible ignored in such a set-up.
They will believe their process is now objective because it's living inside a mathematical model.
Their success relied, in other words, on a community's support of their idea and their ability to command that support.
These algorithms don't make things objective, they make things opaque.
They embed our old cultural problems in new mathematical models, giving us a false badge of objectivity.
The most obvious consequence of using this model, for the companies that use it, is that they'll get more and more people just like the people they already have.
key_insight: Algorithmic hiring tools don't create objectivity—they launder existing biases through mathematical models, making discrimination harder to see while giving it a veneer of scientific legitimacy.
